subtopics.title: Branches of Chemistry
common_misconceptions: Chemistry is only about mixing chemicals in a lab
Organic chemistry only studies living things
subtopics.teaching_note: Emphasize that chemistry is everywhere: in cooking (food chemistry), in medicine (pharmaceutical chemistry), in agriculture (fertilizers), and in industry. Nigerian context: petrochemical industry, soap making, food processing.

subtopics.title: Scientific Method and Laboratory Techniques
common_misconceptions: A hypothesis is the same as a theory
Scientific laws are absolute and unchangeable
subtopics.teaching_note: Nigerian context: Ensure students know laboratory safety - acid spills (use water, then NaHCO₃), fire safety, proper handling of Bunsen burners. Common apparatus: beaker, conical flask, pipette, burette, test tube, evaporating dish.

subtopics.title: Historical Development of Atomic Models
common_misconceptions: Electrons orbit the nucleus like planets (outdated Bohr model)
Atoms are indivisible (they are - into subatomic particles)
subtopics.teaching_note: Emphasize that modern understanding is based on quantum mechanics: electrons exist in probability clouds (orbitals), not fixed orbits. Dalton's theory was modified - atoms CAN be divided and CAN be transformed in nuclear reactions.

subtopics.title: Subatomic Particles and Atomic Properties
common_misconceptions: Atomic mass number is the same as relative atomic mass
All atoms of an element have the same number of neutrons
Mass number includes electrons
subtopics.teaching_note: Key relationships: Atomic number Z = number of protons = number of electrons (in neutral atom). Mass number A = protons + neutrons. Isotopes have same Z but different neutron numbers. RAM = Σ(isotope mass × abundance). Electronic configuration: 2, 8, 8, 18... for Nigerian syllabus.

subtopics.title: Electronic Configuration and the Periodic Table
common_misconceptions: 4s fills before 3d (true but students forget)
Maximum electrons in shell n is 2n² but outermost is 8
Not understanding why configuration affects chemical properties
subtopics.teaching_note: For Nigerian SS1, use simple shell model: K(2), L(8), M(8/18), N(18). Aufbau order: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p, 4s, 3d, 4p. Valence electrons determine group number for main group elements. Period = number of shells.

subtopics.title: Periodic Law and Table Organization
common_misconceptions: Group number equals number of valence electrons for ALL groups (not for transition metals)
Period number equals number of valence electrons
All metals are on the left side
subtopics.teaching_note: Key: Period = horizontal row (7 periods). Group = vertical column (18 groups or 8 main groups in older notation). Group 1 = alkali metals, Group 2 = alkaline earth, Group 17 = halogens, Group 18 = noble gases. Metals on left, non-metals on right, metalloids on staircase.

subtopics.title: Periodic Trends
common_misconceptions: Atomic radius increases across a period (it DECREASES)
Ionization energy decreases down a group (it DECREASES)
Cations are larger than their parent atoms (they are SMALLER)
subtopics.teaching_note: Trends ACROSS period (left to right): atomic radius decreases, ionization energy increases, electronegativity increases, metallic character decreases. Trends DOWN group: atomic radius increases, ionization energy decreases, electronegativity decreases, metallic character increases. Cations smaller, anions larger than parent atoms.

subtopics.title: Ionic (Electrovalent) Bonding
common_misconceptions: Ionic compounds contain molecules (they contain ions in a lattice)
Ionic bonds involve sharing electrons (NO - transfer)
All ionic compounds are soluble in water
subtopics.teaching_note: Ionic bonding: metal loses electrons → cation (+), non-metal gains electrons → anion (-). Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions. Examples: NaCl, MgO, CaCl₂. Properties: high melting point, conduct electricity when molten or aqueous, soluble in water, crystalline structure.

subtopics.title: Covalent Bonding
common_misconceptions: Covalent bonds only form between non-metals (H can form with metals)
All covalent compounds are liquids/gases at room temperature
Polar covalent means ionic
subtopics.teaching_note: Covalent: electron sharing between atoms with similar electronegativity. Non-polar: equal sharing (H₂, Cl₂, CH₄). Polar: unequal sharing (HCl, H₂O). Coordinate: one atom donates both electrons (NH₄⁺). Properties: low melting point (molecular), insoluble in water (usually), don't conduct electricity. Exception: giant covalent (diamond, graphite) have high mp.

subtopics.title: Metallic Bonding and Intermolecular Forces
common_misconceptions: Metallic bonding is the same as covalent bonding
Hydrogen bonding is a covalent bond (it's intermolecular)
Intermolecular forces are as strong as chemical bonds
subtopics.teaching_note: Metallic bond: lattice of positive ions in a sea of delocalized electrons. Properties: good conductivity, malleability, ductility, lustre, high melting point. Intermolecular forces (weaker): van der Waals (all molecules), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), hydrogen bonds (H bonded to N, O, F). H-bonding explains water's anomalous properties.

subtopics.title: The Mole Concept
common_misconceptions: Molar mass and relative molecular mass are the same (different units: g/mol vs no units)
Mole is a mass (it's an amount)
Avogadro's constant is 6.02 (it's 6.02 × 10²³)
subtopics.teaching_note: Key: 1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles (atoms, molecules, ions). Molar mass (g/mol) = RAM or RMM numerically. At STP: 1 mole gas = 22.4 dm³. At RTP: 1 mole gas = 24 dm³. Number of moles = mass/molar mass = volume/molar volume (for gases) = concentration × volume (for solutions).

subtopics.title: Chemical Equations and Calculations
common_misconceptions: Not balancing equations before calculations
Using wrong mole ratio from equation
Confusing theoretical yield with actual yield
subtopics.teaching_note: Calculation steps: 1) Write balanced equation. 2) Identify given and required substances. 3) Convert given to moles. 4) Use mole ratio from equation. 5) Convert moles of required to mass/volume. Percentage yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100. Limiting reagent: the reactant that runs out first.

subtopics.title: Acid-Base Theories
common_misconceptions: All acids are dangerous strong acids
pH can be negative (it can for strong acids)
Bases and alkalis are the same (all alkalis are bases, not all bases are alkalis)
subtopics.teaching_note: Arrhenius: Acid produces H⁺ in water. Base produces OH⁻. Brønsted-Lowry: Acid = proton donor, Base = proton acceptor. Conjugate pair: acid ⇌ base + H⁺. Strong acids: HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃ (fully ionize). Weak acids: CH₃COOH (partially ionize). pH = -log[H⁺]. pH < 7 acidic, pH = 7 neutral, pH > 7 alkaline.

subtopics.title: Salts and Their Preparation
common_misconceptions: All salts are neutral (acid salts are acidic, basic salts are alkaline)
Neutralization always gives pH 7 (depends on strong/weak acid/base combination)
All salts are soluble
subtopics.teaching_note: Preparation methods: 1) Acid + metal (for salts of moderately reactive metals). 2) Acid + base/alkali (neutralization, titration). 3) Acid + carbonate. 4) Direct combination (elements). 5) Double decomposition (precipitation). Solubility rules: All nitrates soluble. Most chlorides soluble (except AgCl, PbCl₂). Most sulfates soluble (except BaSO₄, PbSO₄). Most carbonates INSOLUBLE (except Na, K, NH₄).

subtopics.title: Concepts of Oxidation and Reduction
common_misconceptions: Oxidation always involves oxygen (definition has expanded)
Oxidation number is the same as valency (related but different)
Redox reactions only involve metals
subtopics.teaching_note: Modern definitions: Oxidation = increase in oxidation number (or loss of electrons). Reduction = decrease in oxidation number (or gain of electrons). OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). Oxidizing agent gets reduced. Reducing agent gets oxidized. Common oxidizing agents: KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, H₂O₂, Cl₂.

subtopics.title: Oxidation Numbers and Balancing Redox Equations
common_misconceptions: Oxidation number of oxygen is always -2 (except peroxides, OF₂)
Hydrogen always has +1 oxidation number (in metal hydrides it's -1)
Not balancing both atoms and charges in half equations
subtopics.teaching_note: Rules: Free element = 0. Monatomic ion = charge. H = +1 (except metal hydrides = -1). O = -2 (except peroxides = -1, OF₂ = +2). Group 1 = +1. Group 2 = +2. Sum in compound = 0. Sum in polyatomic ion = charge. To balance redox: identify oxidation number changes, balance electrons lost = electrons gained.
